text: During a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy, the surgeon divides the ileocolic artery and vein at their origin, near the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and vein (SMV). The vessels are visibly separated with traction before division.

The surgeon carefully pushes the duodenum and the fascia covering the kidney downward to enter the correct surgical plane, minimizing the risk of bleeding and protecting vital structures. The duodenum becomes visible as flimsy attachments are dissected to move it further away from the surgical field.

The laparoscopic camera is positioned through the suprapubic port, providing a view from below upwards towards the liver and right shoulder. The surgeon has created three working ports: one in the parumbilical region slightly to the left, another in the right iliac fossa, and a third in the epigastric region towards the left. As the dissection progresses, the duodenum and pancreas become visible in the panoramic view.

The surgeon dissects along the superior mesenteric vessel axis, revealing the head of the pancreas. Using blunt dissection, the surgeon develops the plane and removes flimsy adhesions to the duodenum, pushing it away from the operating field. The mesocolon is dissected from the anterior surface of the pancreas, with the blue vein, identified as the right branch of the middle colic vein, visible to the right of the instrument.

The surgeon dissects the right colic artery and vein, which are located near the lower border of the pancreas. The right colic artery is isolated and ligated using metal clips, followed by division between the clips. The right colic vein is further isolated for secure clip application, adhering to oncological principles for a right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon divides the right colic artery, revealing its lumen, and ligates the right colic vein using clips, dividing it between them. Adhesions on the anterior surface of the pancreas are divided to enter the lesser sac, creating a visible defect. A piece of gauze is placed on the dissected region to mark the entry into the lesser sac, aiding in identifying the space during the laparoscopic right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon continues the dissection below to identify the gonadal vessels and the ureter, using minimal traction to enter the correct planes. The serratus fascia covering the perinephric fat is visible below the dissection area, indicating the correct surgical plane. The texture of the perinephric fat differs from the mesocolic fat. The medial dissection is nearly complete, with clearly visible planes and expanded space achieved through a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques.

The surgeon completes the medial to lateral dissection, revealing the kidney with perinephric fat and the ureter with gonadal vessels covered by fascia. The ascending colon mass is visible, and the lateral peritoneal attachments are incised along the line of Toldt. The patient is positioned with right lateral tilt and head up to optimize the surgical field. The appendix is used for traction to prevent injury, and the assistant provides medial traction to tension the peritoneum. The surgeon uses blunt dissection to open the pre-dissected space, dividing the lateral attachments up to the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon approaches the hepatic flexure, with the liver visible in the field. The lateral attachments near the hepatic flexure are released, facilitating the shift of dissection towards the supracolic compartment. The peritoneal attachment is divided, allowing entry into the correct plane. The gastrocolic omentum is divided to access the lesser sac. The proximal transverse colon is prepared for division as part of the right hemicolectomy, addressing the growth at the beginning of the ascending colon.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the hepatic flexure of the transverse colon, ensuring there is no undue tension for specimen retrieval. The lesser sac is opened, and the peritoneal attachments are further freed to facilitate this process.

The surgeon completes the division of the last attachments to fully mobilize the hepatic flexure of the transverse colon. The transverse colon is now free of any attachments, and the specimen is mobilized without any strands pulling it. Visible anatomical structures include the kidney with perinephric fat and Gerota's fascia, the gallbladder, liver, and the duodenum, which is caucarized. The head of the pancreas is seen medial to the duodenum, and the stomach is visible above. A small fibrous strand is divided, and a gauze piece used earlier in the dissection is visible. A drain is placed subhepatically, and the omentum is pulled down to cover the anastomosis.
